ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for 3-in-1 Thai milk tea; closest defensible match is 'instant tea beverage' (FOODON_03301103). Thai milk tea with creamer and sugar is a more specific product not yet represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code exists for instant 3-in-1 Thai milk tea premix with non-dairy creamer and sugar.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for instant Thai milk tea premix; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

preparation_method: dissolve in hot or cold water
allergens: milk (non-dairy creamer may contain milk derivatives)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one sachet (approximately 20 g powder) dissolved in 180 ml of hot or cold water, yielding roughly 200 ml of prepared beverage.

nutrient_source.title: 3-in-1 Thai Milk Tea — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for '3 in 1 Thai milk tea' (cr_id D09020011). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for non-dairy creamer (USDA SR Legacy #01069 and branded creamer entries) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; value estimated at 1.2 g/100g based on typical palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer fraction at reported fat level of 1.5 g/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 3-in-1 instant Thai milk tea in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 53 based on the closest analogous food: sweetened condensed milk tea / milk tea with sugar, which has a reported GI of approximately 50–55 in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Per standard GI classification (University of Sydney / WHO-FAO 1998), GI 53 falls in the low category (GI ≤ 55 = low; 56–69 = medium; ≥ 70 = high). The high sugar content (13.25 g/100g, predominantly sucrose from added sugar) and liquid form are reflected in the glycemic load rather than the GI category. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × carbohydrate per serving (30.62 g per 200 g serving) / 100 = 16.2. Input record GI of 31 (low, confidence 0.3) from 'Embedded GI Database' was rejected as the source is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (13.25 g/100g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage; insulin-mediated sodium retention and weight gain raise blood pressure independently of the low sodium content (39 mg/100g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage; the 13.25 g/100g sugar load in liquid form drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and contributes to weight gain, both of which elevate blood pressure regardless of the low sodium level, consistent with AHA 2018 guidance on sugar-sweetened beverages and hypertension risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (13.25 g/100g) with zero dietary fibre in a liquid sugar-sweetened beverage; liquid sugars are absorbed faster than equivalent solid-food sugars, causing rapid postprandial glucose excursions.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; the 13.25 g/100g sugar in liquid form with zero fibre causes rapid postprandial glucose excursions, and a single 200 ml serving delivers approximately 26.5 g of sugar with a glycemic load of 16.2, which is clinically significant for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Ultra-processed sugar-sweetened beverage with saturated fat (~1.2 g/100g) from palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer, possible trans fat (0.1 g/100g), and high sugar load; all are independent cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage; the combination of high added sugar (13.25 g/100g), saturated fat from non-dairy creamer (~1.2 g/100g), and possible trans fat (0.1 g/100g) in an ultra-processed liquid form represents a multi-pathway cardiovascular risk, consistent with WHO and AHA dietary guidance for CVD prevention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 3-in-1 Thai Milk Tea (ชาไทยสำเร็จรูป 3 in 1; 三合一泰式奶茶) is an ultra-processed instant beverage premix originating from Thailand, widely consumed across Southeast Asia and available in Singapore supermarkets and convenience stores. Each sachet contains a pre-blended combination of black tea powder (typically Ceylon or Assam tea with Thai-style orange food colouring), non-dairy creamer (usually palm-oil-based), and refined sugar, designed to be dissolved in hot or cold water to produce a sweet, creamy, orange-hued milk tea. The prepared drink has a distinctively sweet, mildly astringent tea flavour with a rich, creamy mouthfeel contributed by the non-dairy creamer. Nutritionally, per 100 g of prepared beverage it provides 79 kcal, 1.25 g protein, 1.5 g fat (predominantly saturated from palm-oil creamer), and 15.31 g carbohydrate of which 13.25 g is sugar, with zero dietary fibre and 39 mg sodium. The high sugar content (13.25 g/100g) in liquid form with zero fibre makes this a high-concern beverage for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease, as liquid sugars are absorbed faster than solid-food equivalents and the non-dairy creamer contributes saturated and possible trans fat. The glycemic load per standard 200 ml serving is approximately 16.2, which is clinically significant despite the low GI of 53. This product is classified as ultra-processed (NOVA Group 4) and is not recommended for regular consumption in any clinical dietary context.
common_user_queries: How much sugar is in 3-in-1 Thai milk tea?
Can diabetics drink Thai milk tea?
Is instant Thai tea high in calories?
What is in a 3-in-1 Thai tea sachet?

How many calories in a cup of Thai milk tea?
Is Thai iced tea high in sugar?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D09020011, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy non-dairy creamer data with documented adaptation. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (sweetened milk tea) with clear documentation. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source; instant tea premix with non-dairy creamer and sugar contains no fibre-bearing ingredients. 2. calcium_mg is 0.0 and iron_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source for this product; non-dairy creamer does not contribute measurable calcium or iron at this serving level. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile for this specific 3-in-1 Thai milk tea premix is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 4. trans_fat_g set to 0.1 g/100g: estimated from typical palm-oil-based non-dairy creamer formulations; partially hydrogenated vegetable oil may be present in some formulations. Consumers should verify product label. 5. potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for this specific instant premix not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 6. Input GI value of 31 (low, confidence 0.3, source 'Embedded GI Database') was rejected as the source is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 policy; GI revised to 53 based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous food evidence. Per standard GI classification (University of Sydney / WHO-FAO 1998), GI 53 is correctly categorised as LOW (GI ≤ 55 = low). gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in iteration 3 to resolve q4 failure. 7. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.2 g/100g from USDA SR Legacy non-dairy creamer data; HPB source does not separately report saturated fat for this item. 8. Thai cuisine: Thai-script local name provided (ชาไทยสำเร็จรูป 3 in 1); Chinese-script name provided for regional alias coverage (三合一泰式奶茶). Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are not applicable as this is a Thai-origin product, not a Singaporean dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft built from HPB source data. Ontology match types set.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and foodex2_match_type from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' per G5 requirements.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index value of 53. Per University of Sydney / WHO-FAO 1998 standard GI classification, GI ≤ 55 = low, 56–69 = medium, ≥ 70 = high. GI 53 is correctly classified as low. Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this correction. This resolves the q4 failure identified in iteration 2 validator feedback.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all hard gates passed and the record scored 8/8.
